1: Psalm 110 is the most frequently quoted OT text in the NT. Jesus cites it in the Synoptics (Matt 22:41-46 and parallels) as a riddle about the Messiah's nature. Peter cites it at Pentecost (Acts 2:34-35). Paul cites it (1 Cor 15:25; Eph 1:20; Col 3:1). Hebrews cites or alludes to it eight times (1:3, 13; 5:6, 10; 6:20; 7:17, 21; 8:1; 10:12-13). Its dominance in NT Christology is due to its dual claim: (1) divine enthronement at God's right hand; (2) eternal Melchizedekian priesthood. These two — divine lordship and perfect priesthood — are the two Christological pillars the NT builds on from this one Psalm.
2: 'The LORD sends forth your mighty scepter from Zion. Rule in the midst of your enemies!' — the divine sending of the royal scepter (matteh uzzecha, the scepter of your strength) 'from Zion' — the temple-city as the locus of divine sovereignty. The king's authority originates from YHWH's sanctuary, not from military conquest. 'Rule in the midst of your enemies' (redeh beqerev oyevecha) — dominion maintained not in safe territory but surrounded by opposition. The Davidic king reigns while enemies remain; the eschatological king will reign until all enemies are subdued (1 Cor 15:25).
3: 'Your people will offer themselves freely on the day of your power, in holy garments; from the womb of the morning, the dew of your youth will be yours' — 'offer themselves freely' (amcha nedavot beyom chelecha) — the volunteer army motif: nedavot (free-will offerings/volunteers) who come willingly on the 'day of power' (the day of battle). 'Holy garments' (behadre qodesh) — the priestly-military language: the armies come as a holy assembly. 'Dew of your youth' (tal yaldutecha) — the mysterious closing image: youthful vigor as fresh as the morning dew, suggesting an inexhaustible supply of warriors, or the king's own eternal youth.
4: 'The LORD has sworn and will not change his mind, You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek' — the most theologically significant verse of the psalm. YHWH swears an oath (nishba YHWH velo yinachem) — the unconditional nature of the Davidic covenant underscored by divine oath. 'Priest forever after the order of Melchizedek' (kohen leolam al divrati Malki-Tzedek) — the king is simultaneously priest, in the lineage of Melchizedek (the pre-Levitical priest-king of Salem: Gen 14:18-20). Hebrews 5-7 develops this text as the definitive argument for Jesus's high priesthood: Melchizedekian priesthood is superior to Levitical priesthood because it is royal, eternal, and not bound to the Levitical genealogy.
5: 'The Lord is at your right hand; he will shatter kings on the day of his wrath' — 'at your right hand' — in v1 the king was invited to sit at YHWH's right hand; here YHWH himself is 'at your right hand' (the defensive, protective position). The relationship is mutual: the king at YHWH's right as honored guest; YHWH at the king's right as protector. 'Shatter kings' (machatz melachim beyom apo) — the divine warrior's judgment on the kings of the nations who resist the Melchizedekian king. The language of eschatological judgment.
6: 'He will execute judgment among the nations, filling them with corpses; he will shatter chiefs over the wide earth' — the cosmic scope of the judgment: 'among the nations' (bagoyim), 'over the wide earth' (rosh al-eretz rabbah) — no geographic limit. 'Filling them with corpses' (male geviyot) — the battlefield carnage of comprehensive victory. 'Shatter chiefs' (machatz rosh) — the destruction of enemy leadership. Rev 19:11-21 (the rider on the white horse, the eschatological victory) draws on this psalm's divine-warrior imagery.
7: 'He will drink from the brook by the way; therefore he will lift up his head' — the psalm's final verse is the most cryptic. 'Drink from the brook by the way' (minachal baderekh yishteh) — possibly an image of the divine warrior pausing briefly mid-campaign to refresh himself, indicating the ease of the victory (no extended siege, no long campaign). 'Lift up his head' (al-ken yarim rosh) — the lifted head is the posture of victory and confidence (cf. Ps 3:3). The psalm that opened with the invitation to sit at YHWH's right hand closes with the triumphant lifting of the head — from the honored seat to the victorious field.
